questions.explanation: In the present simple, he/she/it adds -s to the base verb: She works in a bank. "work" is the base form used with I/you/we/they, "working" is the -ing form (used after be), and "is work" mixes two verbs.
With they (a plural subject), use the base verb with no -s: They live in Tunis. The -s ending ("lives") is only for he/she/it, and "living"/"is living" are not the simple present form.

questions.prompt: What is the correct third-person form of the verb "study"?
questions.explanation: When a verb ends in a consonant + y, change the y to i and add -es: study → studies. So "He studies hard." "studys" keeps the y wrongly, and the other spellings are not real words.
For he/she/it the negative is doesn't + base verb: He doesn't eat meat. "don't" is for I/you/we/they, "doesn't eats" double-marks the -s (it already sits on does), and "not eat" has no auxiliary.

A yes/no question with she uses Does + subject + base verb: Does she play tennis? "Do she" uses the wrong auxiliary, "Does she plays" double-marks the -s, and "She does play?" keeps statement word order.
